sentences.text: Actually-- excuse me.
OK?
I didn't-- I didn't tell you that I was Hannah Wolhendler, but I was Estella Hannah.
So you didn't marked it, but I-- I should say it.
I was born December 25, 1926.
In Poland.
Lodz, Poland.
My father's name was Pinchas Wolhendler, W-O-L-H-E-N-D-L-E-R.
My mother's, Karola Wolhendler.
Her maiden name was Karola Szczupak, C-Z-- C-- C-Z-- S-Z-C-Z-U-P-A-K.
My father was like collecting money from the-- from the-- for the firm over-- all over Poland.
He was sent all over Poland to collect money for the firm he worked for.
She was a housewife.
I had a brother three years older than me.
His name was Moishe [?
Wolf ?] and Wolhendler.
My only grandmother was alive when I was-- when I remember.
Her name was Minka, M-I-N-K-A, Hammer, H-A-M-M-E-R.
The rest, I don't know.
I never met them.
They were not-- they were deceased.
I was born in a-- in a middle class family in a nice, nice section of the town in Lodz.
And I was very protected by my mother as a child.
I had a happy childhood.
My father was a very delicate man and a very loving man.
And as, as I could remember, it always was fights with the siblings, siblings.
My brother and me always fought, but that was in fun.
It was not in tragic.
And we went every year to, to the country for the summer, for vacation.
We came back.
Food was plenty.
We even had a maid.
Vladia, ?] her name was.
She was a country girl, and she lived with us.
I went to school.
My mother was so protective of me that she sent her to take me to school and to pick me up from school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's all I can remember from my childhood.
I was 13 when one day, we heard on the radio about, about Adolf Hitler with his speeches.
I remember everybody was concentrated by the radio to listen to it.
And they used to say, we have to pray because I think we're going to-- we're going to go.
He's going to be after Jews.
I couldn't understand.
I was just 13.
To me, it looked like holiday.
My father didn't go to work.
I didn't have school.
My brother didn't go to school.
Little did I know what a holiday I had because a few-- a few weeks later, Poland was occupied.
And when they came to Lodz with the soldiers, they started to make raids on the Jewish people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They called people to the-- to the-- how to-- how called-- not on the street, but in the-- in the court from the houses, and they selected mostly first of all young people.
They selected this one, that one, just with the finger.
The soldiers looked ferocious.
I remember my mother hid me in a attic.
The worst part of it was that our own neighbors, Polacks, they put a finger on us so we couldn't hide.
But somehow I was hidden, and a lot of people were selected to go with the soldiers.
They never-- we never saw them again.
There were incidents which I remember we have-- we had-- what do you call it-- a gate, a gate, a wooden gate in our-- in the courtyard.
And when they took-- when they-- when they called some people, they had little kids on their arm, little babies.
It's very hard to talk about it to me, but I saw they grabbed the children and threw it over the gate.
Because I was hidden in a attic, it was a little like, like a little light there, and I looked down.
Every day, we were afraid we're going-- we're going to be taken.
Finally, it took, I suppose-- I, I understood people were dying.
They did-- they took the, the Jewish people was-- which had beards, they were religious people.
They cut them off in the street.
They, they, they [? hit ?] them.
It was-- it was very, very bad.
Then it came a big announcement that we all have to go to the ghetto.
I didn't know what the ghetto was, but it was outskirts of town, mostly Jewish populated and poor people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one by one, the buildings were marked to go.
How did we go?
Me and my brother, we had a little like carriage.
We put-- whatever we could, we put in there because we couldn't take much with us.
Leave everything and go to ghetto, to those quarters.
As I remember, on the way, a lot of people were, were dying on the way.
Me and my brother, we were still young.
My mother, my father, we all-- we all started to go to those quarters.
A lot of people, young people, they ran away.
They were on foot.
They ran away to other par-- excuse me-- outer parts of the city.
And they were-- they were trying to get to another country just away from, from Poland.
My father was in between them because they told him, you better go or you're going to get deported and shot anyway.
But it took a day or two.
He came back.
He came back.
He couldn't leave his family.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the ghetto, we, we were given a little room with another family-- very poor family-- to sleep and to eat with them together.
And by the way, before I went to the ghetto, they had like rations, which I forget from one time to another.
We had rations.
We got pieces of bread, special-- Jews got very little to eat.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And over there, we, we tried to be with that family, which was a very bad situation.
We were not used to it.
Matter of fact, that's the first time in my life I got period over there.
And I get so scared, I thought I'm dying until my mother calmed me down and said that's the age.
And we were there in the ghetto.
Then they put this in another part of the city, was an apartment for ourselves.
They took-- my father worked in a place where they distributed food for people.
My mother worked in a-- in a kitchen, in a-- how do call it-- in a collective kitchen.
That's what they called it.
They took me out of schools.
I was a very good student before the war.
I had-- I had scholarship.
I should have gone to what they call the gymnasium, but it was like, I suppose, junior high-- private.
But it was-- everything was cut off.
And they took me to a factory where they made hats for the soldiers, knitting hats.
I learned how to knit.
I was-- suppose I was very good at it.
They kept me there.
They gave us a soup every day.
That was our life.
When I came home, my mother came tired and brought a little from potato skins-- the potato skins, really, so she could make something for us to eat.
My father was still working.
And my brother was hungry, and the piece of bread, what we got for the ration-- many times, he ate it, not left-- not leaving anything for me.
But there were-- they were kids.
I, I probably was angry, but I don't remember too much about it.
Then one day we were-- we were there for four and a half years in the ghetto.
Somehow, it was cold.
We had to blow on the stove to, to, to warm up a little food.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had the rations.

And then we heard that our, our elders, who was-- who the German took for him to manage the ghetto, he had an order to bring around, I don't know, 50 or more little children so they can send them out.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The whole population in the ghetto was so-- was so tragic, sad.
Children, they took it away from their mothers, and they send them out.
Nobody know where.
They never saw them again.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
It was-- it was really tragic.
Every day, a lot of people died from hunger.
They couldn't manage.
They couldn't stay alive.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] It was-- was 19-- I think it was 1943 or something.
They said that we all have to leave the ghetto.
We asked plenty, where we going to go?
Nobody knew.
Nobody knew.
Nobody came back to tell us where they went.
One by one, they selected streets.
They selected people, and they took them away.
My brother were taken away from home with other young men.
It was called like a-- not like a prison, but like a hall where they-- where they took all those young people.
[NON-ENGLISH], it was called.
We all ran, that I remember, my mother, me, to see him, to see, see if-- what they did to them.
We didn't know.
They just got-- the soldiers got them-- I remember my brother's-- my brother's eyes when he looked at me.
And he said, I'm going, but you're staying, like, like he would be jealous.
And this stayed with me all of my life.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know why he said it.
He was-- not he was jealous, but, of course, he didn't know where he was going.
And I was staying with my parents.
They took him away.
We never knew about him-- where he disappeared, where he went, nothing.
Nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went-- they took us on, on a wooden horse and buggies.
They took us to, to a place where they had-- to the train station.
We didn't know where they take us.
My father, mother, and me, we were put in the train like those cattle trains with no windows.
It just was like a big [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] wooden, wooden walls.
And it was little light coming through.
That's all we saw.
And then they took us to a place.
We found out that it was Auschwitz.
A lot of people-- some people died on those trains.
They-- there was no facilities to-- the bathrooms.
Was no food.
And we lay on the floor or on the bench.
And they took us to a big hall in Auschwitz to select us.
In that hall, there were a lot of people.
But before they took us out of that hall, they made a selection like-- I remember like today.
My father came down from the train, my mother.
They could hardly walk from hunger and from exhaustion.
Anyway, the-- they, they, they put a finger on my father to go one way.
Me and my mother, they put on the other side.
I never saw my father again.
He was looking out and the last words he said before we went from the train, he said, stay close to your mother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they took him away.
They took him away with other people.
Me and my mother came to a big, big hall.
They asked us to take everything off, just to be naked, and put it on the floor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That time, somebody told us-- I don't remember, a woman or a man.
They took women on one side, men on the other, that I shouldn't say that I am my mother's daughter because they will push-- put me someplace else.
They would-- they would never let me stay with my mother.
So my mother took her maiden name, but we still were together.
From there, they took us to another room.
They shaved our heads bare.
Matter of fact, I didn't recognize my own mother.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took us again to Auschwitz.
I mean, that-- we were in Auschwitz, but the outskirts-- in, in the camp, there was a camp.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They segregated us.
Some of them were younger.
Some of them were older.
They put us on the-- on the floor, a lot of people in one room.
When we had to go to the bathroom, there were bathrooms outside, like outhouses.
We had to go through the people, every one of us.
And we were there in Auschwitz, then, then they, they-- we couldn't wash.
There was a collective washroom.
And we had-- they called it appels.
Every morning, they took us outside, and they counted us.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after this, we went back.
We got a soup, every one of us.
The soup con-- it consisted of kohlrabi.
I don't know how to call it in English, like a vegetable with water and one slice of bread.
My mother was very weak.
Me, I don't know.
I was young, I suppose.
Didn't bother me that much.
We were there a few weeks like that.
I saw from afar-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I saw the crematorium.
A lot of people were taken there.
And I even saw the, the-- what do you call it?
I can't even talk about it.
[DRINKS] A lot of girls wanted to go-- to run away.
They got caught and killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were there for a few weeks.
From Auschwitz-- I don't know how long, if two weeks, or a month we were there, not working, just being, just existing.
They took us again to a train.
This time they selected people.
This here.
This there.
I don't remember how many they selected.
But luckily, my mother was selected to go where I was.
And they took us to Bergen-Belsen.
Bergen-Belsen was just made a camp.
It was not even finished with the barracks.
So we-- when we arrived on the outskirts of Bergen-Belsen, we had to walk like a few miles to the camp.
I was very weak on the way to Bergen-Belsen.
I was sick, and I had typhus-- diarrhea and fever.
But I walked.
If I would stop, I would be killed.
Finally, we arrived-- a few miles, and we arrived in Bergen-Belsen.
Me, they put in a makeshift hospital.
They put me on straw.
I remember like today.
My mother, poor mother, was crying that I'm dying.
Well, somehow, I came out of it.
Then they were-- the barracks were ready.
They put us in barracks on, on-- what do you call those beds, one on top of the other.
Got to help me remember those names.
Bunk-beds, right, on straw.
And we were there, like, I don't know how many women.
The bathroom facilities was a big, big can-like in the back.
We all had to use it.
I was very sick.
I even were delirious.
I, I told everybody-- how do I know about it is one of the girls survived, and I saw her afterwards, after the war.
She told me I gave everybody food.
I didn't know what I was saying, but I rationed everybody food because I was delirious.
I had so much fever.
And they took me to a hospital.
That time, there was a-- there was a, a barrack, like they called it, and they called it the hospital.
They, they had a nurse of our people, a girl who was a nurse.
And then it was a German doctor.
And I was there in barrack for a nice few months in Bergen-Belsen.
My mother was in Bergen-Belsen too, and they did, every day, selections.
They took-- they took people to work.
She hid in the bathroom because she didn't want to leave me alone.
They didn't catch her but she finally got sick and tub-- tuberculosis because there was no food.
And she came to the same hospital as I was.
I got well, well enough that they saw that I am well.
I had to help with the-- with the chores.
I had to take out the big can-- what we made, you know, what we relieved ourselves-- out.
Then they took me to help out to take the dead bodies with another girl outside in a big ditch.
I never forget that.
There were I don't know how many people laying there already.
They took-- everybody who died, they took to another room there where we used to-- the washroom, and they put them on the floor.
And then they took us girls, one by the legs and one by the arms, to take them to that ditch.
There was a quite a while until my mother got so sick that she died.
I didn't take her out, but I can imagine.
And I was running around everybody to say, my mother is going-- is, is going to die.
My mother is going to die.
One doctor that was a woman, our people-- she didn't have a choice, I suppose-- she said, where's she going?
I was-- I was there when she died.
They probably took her out in the same place.
Now I was in Bergen-Belsen for quite a while.
Up till 1944-- I don't remember the date.
I don't.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] All of a sudden, I couldn't walk because I was so weak from those typhuses.
But all the girls and everybody went out to see the English peo-- soldiers to come to free us.
They called them the Tommys.
I went out holding-- I remember holding to the wall.
I was very skinny, and I couldn't walk.
And we were so happy to see them.
They, they did what they could.
They saw what happened.
They wanted to give us food, so they gave us whatever rations they had in the-- in the trucks.
There was cans.
They meant well, but a lot of our people died.
They were not used to digest it.
All of a sudden, well, we were very happy that we, we heard the Germans are in the woods.
They are running away.
They are going.
And a lot of-- a lot of girls went out running, and, and, and I couldn't run.
We just saw one incident which I was really happy to see.
It was a, a truck with German SS girls.
They hi left and right everybody.
And Irma Grese was on it.
Irma Grese is very-- is very known even after the war.
In [?
Luneburg, ?] they had her.
And she was ugly as a-- as night.
And she had a bleeding hole in her head.
I remember one English soldier ask, please is there somebody, a nurse here?
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And one of the girls who was in that hospital who was a nurse.
I am a nurse.
So he says, could you-- could you bandage her?
She says, yeah, but I have to cut her hair off.
She had long blond hair.
And the soldier said, I wish I could, but I have orders to bring her in one piece.
That was the only thing I saw when the Englishmen came in.
